insights: Incident distribution across the five categories—Database, Hardware, Inquiry / Help, Software, and Network—is perfectly uniform, with exactly 100 incidents assigned to each, indicating no categorical imbalance in assignment volume.

The uniformity extends beyond categories to all major assignment dimensions: assigned_to, assignment_group, location, and priority—all show exact proportional distribution with no concentration or shortfall in any segment.

The absence of discrepancy in category distribution contradicts the initial business assumption of imbalance, confirming that assignment logic or intake processes are operating with strict structural parity across categories.
With zero missing values in key assignment fields and a clean 500-row dataset, the observed uniformity is data-verified and not attributable to reporting gaps or data quality issues.

This perfect balance implies that category-level resource allocation decisions (e.g., staffing, SLA tuning, escalation routing) cannot be justified by differential incident volume—and any performance variance across categories must stem from factors other than assignment load.
summary: The analysis confirms there is no discrepancy or imbalance in the distribution of incidents across categories—each of the five categories (Database, Hardware, Inquiry / Help, Software, Network) receives exactly 100 incidents, and this uniformity holds consistently across assignees, groups, locations, and priority levels. The dataset is complete and clean, validating that the observed parity reflects operational reality rather than data artifact. This null result refutes the premise of category-based assignment skew and shifts focus toward investigating non-volume drivers—such as resolution complexity, handling time, or closure rate differences—as the true sources of potential operational disparity. Next, diagnostic analysis should target category-level performance metrics (e.g., MTTR, first-contact resolution, reassignment rate) to identify where imbalances actually manifest.
